content.text: SOC 2 was designed for cloud service providers processing customer data, not for AI systems that generate outputs, make decisions, or interact with sensitive information through natural language. Mapping the five Trust Service Criteria (TSC) categories to GenAI-specific controls requires re-interpreting each criterion's intent in the context of model behavior, prompt handling, and inference operations. The result is a control library that is both auditable under the standard SOC 2 framework and genuinely relevant to the risks that AI systems introduce.

This sub-subtopic explains how each TSC category applies to GenAI systems, what controls satisfy each category, what evidence auditors will request, and how to write control narratives that pass SOC 2 scrutiny. The mapping is practical and grounded in what external auditors actually ask for when they encounter AI systems in a SOC 2 audit scope.
Why TSC Mapping Matters for GenAI

SOC 2 auditors are increasingly encountering AI systems in audit scopes, but the trust service criteria were authored before large language models became production infrastructure. This creates an interpretation gap: an auditor may know what CC6.1 (logical and physical access) means for a database, but may not know how to evaluate it for an LLM API endpoint that accepts natural language inputs. By proactively mapping your GenAI controls to TSC references, you give the auditor a clear framework rather than letting them improvise an evaluation methodology that may be harsher or more inconsistent than necessary.

The mapping also forces internal discipline. When a team must ask 'how does our output monitoring satisfy CC7.2?' they discover gaps they had not noticed. This is the same function that mock audits serve, but the TSC mapping exercise is lighter-weight and can be done at any point in the program's maturity.

attrs.alt: SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria mapped to GenAI controls across Security, Availability, Confidentiality, Processing Integrity, and Privacy categories

content.text: Benefits
Provides a structured vocabulary for communicating AI controls to external auditors who are familiar with SOC 2 but not AI governance.
Surfaces control gaps by forcing each TSC criterion to be satisfied with a concrete AI-specific control rather than a generic IT control.
Enables control reuse across multiple compliance frameworks — a prompt logging control may satisfy both CC7.2 and GDPR Article 30.
Simplifies evidence collection by pre-mapping which artifacts (logs, configs, test results) satisfy which criteria.
Demonstrates compliance program maturity to customers who request SOC 2 reports as part of vendor due diligence.
Trade-offs

SOC 2 was not designed for AI decision-making risks; over-reliance on the mapping may create a false sense of comprehensive coverage where AI-specific risks (bias, hallucination) are not fully addressed.
Auditor interpretation of TSC criteria in AI contexts varies by firm; the mapping must be discussed with the specific auditor to align expectations.
Adding AI-specific controls to SOC 2 scope increases audit cost and effort if the auditor is not already familiar with AI systems.
Processing Integrity (PI) controls for AI outputs are harder to satisfy than traditional PI controls because AI accuracy is probabilistic, not deterministic.
How the Mapping Works: Core Mechanics

Each TSC category contains a set of Common Criteria (CC) points, identified by number (e.g., CC6.1). Each criterion has a stated objective. The mapping exercise asks: 'What control does our AI system have that satisfies this objective, and what evidence demonstrates that the control operates effectively?'

The mapping produces a three-column structure: TSC Criterion → AI Control → Evidence. This structure becomes the control narrative that auditors review. Each criterion should map to at least one preventive control (configuration, policy, architecture) and one detective control (log, alert, test result) demonstrating operating effectiveness over the audit period.
CC: Security — The Largest Mapping Surface
The Common Criteria (CC) category applies to all SOC 2 engagements. For GenAI systems, the most significant CC sub-criteria are CC6, CC7, and CC8.
CC6: Logical and Physical Access Controls

CC6.1 requires that access to the system is restricted to authorized users. For GenAI systems, this means: API keys for model endpoints must be rotated on a defined schedule; access to the model serving infrastructure must be role-based; and production prompt templates must have version-controlled access, preventing unauthorized modification.

CC6.8, which covers protections against unauthorized software and malicious code, is where prompt injection defense sits. An auditor evaluating CC6.8 for a GenAI system should see: a policy defining prompt injection as a threat; evidence of input sanitization or guardrail implementation; and red-team test results demonstrating that injection defenses operate as designed.
CC7: System Operations

CC7.2 covers system monitoring, including alerts and anomaly detection. For GenAI, this maps to: output monitoring for unusual content patterns; alert queues for anomalous inference volumes; and documented SLAs for alert review. The evidence auditors will request includes monitoring dashboards, alert history exports, and incident tickets created from model anomalies.
CC8: Change Management

CC8.1 requires that changes to the system are authorized, tested, and documented. Model retraining is a system change. Every retraining cycle must have: a change ticket with documented justification; test results showing the retrained model performs within defined accuracy thresholds; rollback procedures; and sign-off from an authorized approver before production deployment.
A: Availability

Availability (A1) controls address whether the system performs its functions without interruption. For GenAI, availability risks include: token quota exhaustion on the underlying LLM API; upstream provider outages; and inference infrastructure failures.

Controls that satisfy A1 include: rate limit monitoring with alerting; defined fallback behavior when the primary model endpoint is unavailable (degraded mode, cached responses, or human handoff); capacity testing at peak load; and documented uptime SLAs with evidence of meeting them over the audit period.
C: Confidentiality

Confidentiality (C1) controls protect information that is designated as confidential. GenAI systems present unique confidentiality risks: prompts may contain customer PII or trade secrets; outputs may inadvertently reveal confidential information from training data or the context window; and logging of prompts creates a new confidential data store.

Controls include: DLP scanning of prompts before sending to the model; a prohibition on using production data for model fine-tuning without explicit DLP clearance; encryption of prompt logs at rest; and an output classification review for high-risk use cases.
PI: Processing Integrity

Processing Integrity (PI1) requires that system processing is complete, accurate, timely, and authorized. For AI systems, this is the most challenging category because AI outputs are probabilistic. The standard requires that the system do what it is supposed to do — it does not require perfection, but it does require that accuracy is measured and that errors are detected and corrected.

Controls for PI1 include: accuracy benchmarks defined at deployment time; ongoing accuracy sampling against a test dataset with defined minimum thresholds; hallucination rate monitoring for factual claims; human-in-the-loop review requirements for high-stakes outputs; and an authorized user list for initiating AI inference on sensitive workflows.
P: Privacy

Privacy (P) criteria apply when the AI system processes personal information. Key sub-criteria for GenAI: P3.2 (use of personal information is consistent with the stated purpose) maps to prompt purpose limitation — if the AI is deployed for customer support, prompts should not be routed through it for marketing profiling; P6.3 (retention and disposal of personal information) maps to prompt log retention policies, including automated deletion after the defined retention period.
Mental Model: The Filter Analogy

Think of SOC 2 TSC criteria as a set of filters, each designed to catch a specific category of risk. The Security filters catch access and change management risks. The Availability filter catches uptime risks. The Confidentiality filter catches data exposure risks. Processing Integrity catches accuracy risks. Privacy catches personal data misuse risks. Your job when mapping GenAI controls is to prove that each filter is installed and working — not just that the hardware rack has security bolts on it, but that you can demonstrate water actually passed through the filter and was cleaned.
Implementation: Writing Control Narratives

# soc2-genai-control-mapping.yaml
# Maps SOC 2 Trust Service Criteria to GenAI-specific controls.
# Use this as the input to your auditor's control narrative document.

controls:
  - tsc_reference: CC6.1
    tsc_category: Security
    control_title: "Model API Access Control"
    control_description: >
      Access to production model API endpoints is restricted to authorized 
      service accounts via API key authentication. Keys are rotated every 
      90 days. Access is granted via role-based access control managed in 
      the central IAM system. Access reviews are conducted quarterly.
    control_type: preventive
    evidence_items:
      - "IAM role policy export showing model API permissions"
      - "API key rotation log for the audit period"
      - "Quarterly access review sign-off records"
    control_owner: Platform Security Team
    last_tested: 2026-06-30

  - tsc_reference: CC6.8
    tsc_category: Security
    control_title: "Prompt Injection Defense"
    control_description: >
      All user-supplied input to production AI agents is processed through 
      an input guardrail layer that detects and blocks prompt injection 
      patterns before the payload reaches the model. Guardrail bypass 
      attempts are logged and trigger a security alert.
    control_type: preventive
    evidence_items:
      - "Guardrail configuration in version-controlled IaC repository"
      - "Red-team test report from most recent quarterly test"
      - "Security alert logs showing triggered injection detection events"
    control_owner: AI Security Team
    last_tested: 2026-05-15

  - tsc_reference: CC7.2
    tsc_category: Security
    control_title: "AI Output Anomaly Monitoring"
    control_description: >
      Production AI system outputs are monitored for anomalous content 
      patterns (toxicity, unexpected refusals, hallucination rate spikes) 
      using automated evaluation. Alerts fire when metrics exceed defined 
      thresholds. On-call engineers review and resolve alerts within 4 hours.
    control_type: detective
    evidence_items:
      - "Monitoring dashboard configuration"
      - "Alert history export for the audit period"
      - "On-call runbook with escalation procedures"
      - "Incident tickets created from model alerts (sample)"
    control_owner: AI Platform Team
    last_tested: 2026-07-01

  - tsc_reference: PI1.1
    tsc_category: Processing Integrity
    control_title: "Model Output Accuracy Sampling"
    control_description: >
      A random 2% sample of AI outputs is human-reviewed weekly against 
      an accuracy rubric. Results are recorded in the AI Quality Log. 
      If accuracy falls below 95%, a model performance review is triggered 
      and the incident is escalated to the Model Risk Committee.
    control_type: detective
    evidence_items:
      - "AI Quality Log for the audit period"
      - "Accuracy rubric document"
      - "Sample of completed human review records"
      - "Model performance review records (if triggered)"
    control_owner: Model Quality Team
    last_tested: 2026-07-15

  - tsc_reference: P6.3
    tsc_category: Privacy
    control_title: "Prompt Log Retention and Deletion"
    control_description: >
      Prompt logs containing personal information are retained for 12 months 
      and then automatically deleted by a scheduled job. Manual deletion 
      requests submitted under GDPR Article 17 are processed within 30 days. 
      Deletion completion is recorded in the deletion log.
    control_type: preventive
    evidence_items:
      - "Prompt log retention policy"
      - "Automated deletion job configuration and execution logs"
      - "Sample deletion request records with completion dates"
    control_owner: Privacy Team
    last_tested: 2026-06-01
Code Example: Evidence Collection Script

"""
Collects evidence artifacts for SOC 2 GenAI control mapping.
Produces a structured evidence package for auditor review.
"""
import json, datetime, boto3, hashlib, pathlib

def sha256_file(path: str) -> str:
    h = hashlib.sha256()
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(65536), b""):
            h.update(chunk)
    return h.hexdigest()

def collect_iam_evidence(role_name: str, output_dir: str) -> dict:
    iam = boto3.client("iam")
    policy = iam.get_role(RoleName=role_name)["Role"]
    out_path = pathlib.Path(output_dir) / f"iam_role_{role_name}.json"
    out_path.write_text(json.dumps(policy, default=str, indent=2))
    return {
        "control": "CC6.1",
        "artifact": str(out_path),
        "sha256": sha256_file(str(out_path)),
        "collected_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
    }

def collect_alert_history(cloudwatch_alarm_names: list, output_dir: str) -> dict:
    cw = boto3.client("cloudwatch")
    results = []
    for alarm in cloudwatch_alarm_names:
        history = cw.describe_alarm_history(
            AlarmName=alarm,
            HistoryItemType="StateUpdate",
            MaxRecords=100,
        )["AlarmHistoryItems"]
        results.extend(history)
    out_path = pathlib.Path(output_dir) / "alert_history.json"
    out_path.write_text(json.dumps(results, default=str, indent=2))
    return {
        "control": "CC7.2",
        "artifact": str(out_path),
        "sha256": sha256_file(str(out_path)),
        "collected_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
    }

# Usage
evidence_dir = "/tmp/soc2-evidence-2026-q3"
pathlib.Path(evidence_dir).mkdir(exist_ok=True)
artifacts = [
    collect_iam_evidence("ai-model-service-role", evidence_dir),
    collect_alert_history(["ai-output-anomaly-alarm", "ai-error-rate-alarm"], evidence_dir),
]
manifest = {"evidence_package": artifacts, "prepared_for_audit": "SOC2-2026-Q3"}
pathlib.Path(evidence_dir + "/manifest.json").write_text(json.dumps(manifest, indent=2))
print(f"Evidence package ready at {evidence_dir}/manifest.json")
Real-World Example: SaaS Analytics Platform

A SaaS analytics company integrated an AI-powered report generation feature that sent customer data extracts to an LLM API to produce natural language summaries. Their SOC 2 auditor questioned whether the existing CC6.1 control (which covered their primary database) extended to the LLM API. The team documented: the LLM API key was rotated quarterly and stored in their secrets manager (satisfying CC6.1 design); key rotation logs from the secrets manager audit trail covered the full 12-month audit period (satisfying CC6.1 operating effectiveness); and the LLM vendor's data processing agreement confirmed that prompt data was not used for training (satisfying C1.1). This documentation was accepted without a finding.
Common Mistakes
Mapping Only to Security Criteria

Teams often map AI controls to CC criteria (Security) and stop there, treating Availability, Confidentiality, PI, and Privacy as not applicable to their AI system. Auditors will probe all five TSC categories if personal data flows through the AI system. Build the full mapping even if some categories have fewer controls.
Relying on Vendor SOC 2 Reports as Your Own Controls

The LLM API provider may have a SOC 2 report. That report covers their system, not yours. Your control narrative must address how you use their service securely — key management, prompt content classification, output validation — not just reference their report. Your controls are additive to your vendor's.
Best Practices

Map every TSC criterion to at least two pieces of evidence: one design artifact (policy, configuration) and one operating effectiveness artifact (log, test result, review record) covering the full audit period.
Use the TSC criterion numbering (CC6.1, P6.3) as headings in your control narrative so auditors can navigate directly to the controls they are testing.
Engage your auditor in a pre-audit walkthrough of your GenAI control mapping — auditors appreciate teams that proactively explain their novel system types.
Maintain a control-to-evidence register in your GRC tool so evidence can be pulled on demand during fieldwork without a scramble.

When processing integrity is probabilistic (AI accuracy), document the thresholds, measurement methodology, and remediation triggers — this shows the system has guardrails even if outputs are not 100% accurate.
Keep the control narrative concise and factual; auditors read hundreds of narratives and value clarity over length.
Performance and Scalability

The control mapping itself is a living document. Every time a new AI system is brought into scope, a new model version is deployed, or a new LLM API integration is added, the mapping must be reviewed for new control requirements. A mature program maintains the mapping in a GRC tool (Vanta, Drata, ServiceNow GRC) where control updates trigger automated evidence requests rather than requiring manual process.
Comparison with Alternatives
Framework
AI Coverage
Auditor Familiarity
Effort
Best Use
SOC 2 TSC Mapping
Moderate — requires GenAI interpretation
Very High — universal in cloud services
Medium
SaaS/cloud AI services with B2B customers
Very High — AI-native framework
Growing — newer standard
High
Enterprise AI governance programs
NIST AI RMF
Very High — AI-native
Moderate — US-centric
High
US federal and regulated industries
EU AI Act compliance
High — risk-tier based
Low — newer regulation
Very High
AI systems deployed in the EU
Security and Reliability

The control mapping document itself is security-sensitive: it describes gaps and weaknesses in your control environment. Restrict access to the working mapping to the compliance team, CISO, and control owners. The final auditor-facing control narrative, which describes only the controls (not the gaps), can be shared more broadly. Never include the gap analysis in the artifact submitted to auditors.
Key Takeaways
All five SOC 2 TSC categories apply to GenAI systems when personal data is processed — not just the Security (CC) category.
CC6 maps to API key management, RBAC, and prompt injection defense; CC7 maps to output monitoring and alert SLAs; CC8 maps to model change management.
Processing Integrity controls for AI are probabilistic — document accuracy thresholds, measurement methodology, and remediation triggers rather than claiming deterministic correctness.
Privacy controls must address prompt log retention, purpose limitation, and deletion-on-request workflows that did not exist before AI systems were in scope.
Map each control to at least two evidence artifacts: a design artifact and an operating effectiveness artifact covering the full audit period.
Engage the auditor early in a pre-audit walkthrough of your GenAI control mapping to align interpretation before fieldwork begins.
Vendor SOC 2 reports cover the vendor's environment; your mapping must document the controls you operate on top of their services.
